tossups.question: Franco Sacchetti recounts how after being commissioned to paint a shield with a coat of arms, this artist instead painted it "armed to the teeth." This artist depicted a donor holding the very triptych he was depicted in in one of the earliest examples of the Droste effect. By showing expressions in moments of action in paintings such as Saint Francis Receiving the Stigmata, this artist broke from the Byzantine style. This painter's version of the Lamentation is set on a hill with angels flying above and is on the north wall of a building which includes grisaille panels of the vices and virtues. This artist allegedly drew a perfect circle for Pope Benedict XI ("the eleventh") without the aid of a compass. This student of Cimabue painted the walls of a chapel in Padua commissioned by Enrico Scrovegni. For ten points, name this Proto-Renaissance painter who painted the Arena Chapel.
tossups.question_sanitized: Franco Sacchetti recounts how after being commissioned to paint a shield with a coat of arms, this artist instead painted it "armed to the teeth." This artist depicted a donor holding the very triptych he was depicted in in one of the earliest examples of the Droste effect. By showing expressions in moments of action in paintings such as Saint Francis Receiving the Stigmata, this artist broke from the Byzantine style. This painter's version of the Lamentation is set on a hill with angels flying above and is on the north wall of a building which includes grisaille panels of the vices and virtues. This artist allegedly drew a perfect circle for Pope Benedict XI ("the eleventh") without the aid of a compass. This student of Cimabue painted the walls of a chapel in Padua commissioned by Enrico Scrovegni. For ten points, name this Proto-Renaissance painter who painted the Arena Chapel.

tossups.question: When accused of having ugly children, this painter replied “I make my pictures by day, and my babies by night.” In a fresco by this man, the title figure is depicted expelling demons from the city of Arezzo. Opposing traits like “Desperation” and “Charity” are featured in one fresco of seven virtues and vices by this man. The autobiography of Lorenzo Ghiberti claims that this man painted the (*) Ognissanti Madonna. This artist is allegedly buried next to the campanile he designed for the Florence Cathedral. This man’s Lamentation demonstrates a break from the Byzantine tradition of his mentor Cimabue. Enrico Scrovegni commissioned this artist to paint a series of works including his Kiss of Judas. For 10 points, name this proto-Renaissance painter of the Arena Chapel.
tossups.question_sanitized: When accused of having ugly children, this painter replied "I make my pictures by day, and my babies by night." In a fresco by this man, the title figure is depicted expelling demons from the city of Arezzo. Opposing traits like "Desperation" and "Charity" are featured in one fresco of seven virtues and vices by this man. The autobiography of Lorenzo Ghiberti claims that this man painted the (*) Ognissanti Madonna. This artist is allegedly buried next to the campanile he designed for the Florence Cathedral. This man's Lamentation demonstrates a break from the Byzantine tradition of his mentor Cimabue. Enrico Scrovegni commissioned this artist to paint a series of works including his Kiss of Judas. For 10 points, name this proto-Renaissance painter of the Arena Chapel.

tossups.question: This artist painted multiple versions of the first known stigmatization, where the saint receives the wounds from Christ depicted as a seraphim. As part of a sequence of frescoes of the same saint, this artist depicts that saint surviving a trial by fire before a Sultan. Those frescoes are in the Upper Church of Assisi, while elsewhere this person was the architect of a polychrome campanile, though he died after only the lower stories were built and he is buried according to Vasari, beside it, in Florence’s Duomo. His masterpiece is a fresco cycle depicting the lives of Mary and of Christ and for 10 points, name this artist who decorated the Scrovegni Chapel in Padua.
tossups.question_sanitized: This artist painted multiple versions of the first known stigmatization, where the saint receives the wounds from Christ depicted as a seraphim. As part of a sequence of frescoes of the same saint, this artist depicts that saint surviving a trial by fire before a Sultan. Those frescoes are in the Upper Church of Assisi, while elsewhere this person was the architect of a polychrome campanile, though he died after only the lower stories were built and he is buried according to Vasari, beside it, in Florence's Duomo. His masterpiece is a fresco cycle depicting the lives of Mary and of Christ and for 10 points, name this artist who decorated the Scrovegni Chapel in Padua.

tossups.question: Because of this painting’s use of perspective, the wooden side frames of its central object create miniature panels; through these panels, two bearded saints - who lack halos, unlike the other figures in this painting - gaze at the central scene. This painting’s attribution to its artist is suggested by a 1418 document, but only confirmed in the autobiography of Lorenzo Ghiberti. In this painting, vases with lilies and roses are found in the hands of the two angels in white; those angels kneel on both sides of the steps leading to this painting’s central throne. Since it looks (*) three-dimensional, this painting is a superior version of the same scene shown in the paintings it is displayed next to in the Uffizi: the Rucellai Madonna painted by Duccio and the the Santa Trinita Maesta created by the artist’s teacher Cimabue. For 10 points, identify this scene of the Madonna and Child by Giotto, which is named for a Florentine church dedicated to all saints.
tossups.answer: Ognissanti Madonna [or Giotto’s Madonna Enthroned until “Giotto” is read; prompt on Madonna Enthroned]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Ognissanti Madonna [or Giotto's Madonna Enthroned until "Giotto" is read; prompt on Madonna Enthroned]
tossups.question_sanitized: Because of this painting's use of perspective, the wooden side frames of its central object create miniature panels; through these panels, two bearded saints - who lack halos, unlike the other figures in this painting - gaze at the central scene. This painting's attribution to its artist is suggested by a 1418 document, but only confirmed in the autobiography of Lorenzo Ghiberti. In this painting, vases with lilies and roses are found in the hands of the two angels in white; those angels kneel on both sides of the steps leading to this painting's central throne. Since it looks (*) three-dimensional, this painting is a superior version of the same scene shown in the paintings it is displayed next to in the Uffizi: the Rucellai Madonna painted by Duccio and the the Santa Trinita Maesta created by the artist's teacher Cimabue. For 10 points, identify this scene of the Madonna and Child by Giotto, which is named for a Florentine church dedicated to all saints.

tossups.question: This artist’s success did not depend on his ability to express ideal beauty or realistic scenes, but rather his ability to submit metaphor to narration, according to an extended passage about him in Der Cicerone. A book subtitled for the “humanist observers” of painting is titled for this artist "and the Orators." Susskind burns a chapter of criticism about this artist written by a professor named Fidelman at the end of Bernard Malamud’s story "The Last Mohican." A book by Andrew Ladis argues against a narrative understanding of this artist’s best-known work, and takes its title from a story in which this artist freehanded a perfect (*) circle to gain the commission for that work from Benedict XI. Vasari recounted a story in which this artist painted an extremely lifelike fly onto a painting by his teacher Cimabue. For 10 points, name this artist often considered to be the first Renaissance painter, who did some interior decoration for the Scrovegni Chapel.
tossups.answer: Giotto di Bondone [accept Giotto and the Orators; accept Giotto’s O]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Giotto di Bondone [accept Giotto and the Orators; accept Giotto's O]

Susskind burns a chapter of criticism about this artist written by a professor named Fidelman at the end of Bernard Malamud's story "The Last Mohican." A book by Andrew Ladis argues against a narrative understanding of this artist's best-known work, and takes its title from a story in which this artist freehanded a perfect (*) circle to gain the commission for that work from Benedict XI. Vasari recounted a story in which this artist painted an extremely lifelike fly onto a painting by his teacher Cimabue. For 10 points, name this artist often considered to be the first Renaissance painter, who did some interior decoration for the Scrovegni Chapel.

bonuses.leadin: In October 2019, a panel depicting the mocking of Christ from an altarpiece by this artist was discovered hanging in an elderly French woman’s kitchen. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In October 2019, a panel depicting the mocking of Christ from an altarpiece by this artist was discovered hanging in an elderly French woman's kitchen. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this artist of the Diptych of Devotion, of which only three of its original eight panels have been found. This Florentine artist also created the Santa Trinita Maestà and taught Giotto.

One of these objects created by Cimabue (“chee-mah-BOO-ay”) for a basilica in Florence was damaged by a flood in 1966. Artworks of the Deposition depict Christ being taken down from one of these objects after his death.

A Cimabue (“chee-mah-BOO-ay”) crucifixion in this building has decayed so much that it now resembles a photographic negative. The Master of St. Cecilia may have helped create a series of 28 frescoes in this building depicting its namesake.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about Giorgio Vasari’s artist biographies in The Lives. For 10 points each:
parts: Vasari said that “there was no uglier man in the city of Florence” than this artist. This painter of the Scrovegni Chapel walls once painted a fly so realistic that his teacher Cimabue tried to brush it off.

Vasari’s claim that this painter was blind by age 60 is often disputed. This painter from Sansepolcro depicted a scene from the passion narrative in which three men stand unaware of the main action, paralleling another painting by him in which four men sleep at the base of a tomb.
The first biography in the second volume praises this painter’s famed depiction of the Last Supper. Vasari also celebrates the ambiguous smile in this painter’s portrait of Mona Lisa.
answers: Piero della Francesca [accept either underlined portion; prompt on di Benedetto] (The paintings are Flagellation of Christ and The Resurrection, respectively.)
answers_sanitized: Giotto di Bondone [or Giottus]
Piero della Francesca [accept either underlined portion; prompt on di Benedetto] (The paintings are Flagellation of Christ and The Resurrection, respectively.)
Leonardo da Vinci [or Leonardo da Vinci]

bonuses.leadin: In a recent essay, Jesse McCarthy wrote about wanting more out of this artist’s paintings. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this artist who appears on the cover of McCarthy’s collection Who Will Pay Reparations on My Soul? He’s not Caravaggio, but this artist included a self portrait holding a piece of paper with his signature on it on the far left of his The Calling of Saint Matthew.
Juan de Pareja spent much of his life enslaved by this Spanish artist. This artist freed Pareja in 1650, shortly before beginning work on his masterpiece Las Meninas.

One of the few surviving paintings by Pareja depicts the flight into Egypt. This early renaissance master included a depiction of the flight into Egypt as part of his fresco cycle of the life of Christ in the Arena Chapel.
answers: Diego Velázquez [or Diego Rodríguez de Silva y Velázquez]
answers_sanitized: Juan de Pareja ("puh-RAY-huh")
Diego Velazquez [or Diego Rodriguez de Silva y Velazquez]
Giotto di Bondone [or Giottus]

bonuses.leadin: The white marble colonnades that adorn the façade of this city's cathedral exemplify its distinctive Romanesque style. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Italian city, whose Piazza dei Miracoli also has a campanile (CAMP-ah-NEE-lay), or bell-tower, with an iconic four-degree lean.

For the Baptistery of Pisa, Nicola Pisano carved a hexagonal proto-Renaissance version of one of these structures out of marble. Nicola's son Giovanni omitted a wood board called a tester over one of these structures inspired by Roman sarcophagi for the Church of Sant'Andrea.
The campanile next to Florence Cathedral was designed by this artist, who covered its exterior in geometric decorations of polychrome marble. He also painted a fresco cycle for the Scrovegni Chapel.
answers_sanitized: Pisa
pulpit
Giotto [or Giotto di Bondone or Giottus]

bonuses.leadin: While visiting this building, Dante allegedly asked how its artist could paint beautiful pictures and yet have ugly children, to which the artist retorted that he made his children at night. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this building, which probably contains a portrait of Dante in the “heaven” part of its fresco of the Last Judgment. This building also contains monochrome frescoes depicting seven pairs of opposing virtues and vices.
In the Purgatorio, Dante noted that this painter of the Scrovegni Chapel severely overshadowed his teacher, Cimabue (“chee-mah-BOO-ay”).

A portrait often attributed to Giotto is an early depiction of Dante mainly wearing this color, as he has since traditionally been depicted. Renaissance painters often used this primary color’s pigment vermilion.

bonuses.leadin: Giuliani Pisani proposed that the depiction of a one-horned, snake-vomiting Envy should be paired with Charity in his analysis of fourteen grisaille (“griz-EYE”) figures found in this building. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this building whose vices and virtues are thought to omit avarice, potentially because of a patron’s reputation for usury. This chapel’s blue ceiling depicts stars and four prophets around the Madonna and Child.
Minerva expels the Vices from one of these places in a Triumph of the Virtues by Mantegna. An angel drives Adam and Eve from one of these places in a Massacio fresco.

Vice hides a dagger and sphinx behind her skirt and struggles with Virtue over this figure in a Paolo Veronese painting. Annibale Carracci (“ah-NEE-ba-lay ka-RAH-chee”) also depicted this figure’s choice between allegorical female figures of Vice and Virtue.
answers: Scrovegni (“scro-VAIN-yee”) Chapel [or Cappella degli Scrovegni; or Arena Chapel] (Pisani wrote I volti segreti di Giotto, in which he argued that each vice should be paired with its opposite virtue, rather than going straight through each set.)
gardens [accept Minerva Expelling the Vices from the Garden of Virtues or The Expulsion from the Garden of Eden]
Hercules [or Heracles; accept The Choice of Hercules or the judgment of Hercules or Hercules at the crossroads]

packet.name: N - Northwestern A, NYU A, McGill A, Rutgers A, VCU A, Cambridge B

gardens [accept Minerva Expelling the Vices from the Garden of Virtues or The Expulsion from the Garden of Eden]
Hercules [or Heracles; accept The Choice of Hercules or the judgment of Hercules or Hercules at the crossroads]

bonuses.leadin: Along with his biography of Bernini, the Vasari-wannabe Filippo Baldinucci is best known for a dictionary of artists that begins with this artist. For 10 points each:

That pupil of Cimabue was this Proto-Renaissance artist and circle-drawing enthusiast, who created fresco cycles depicting the Life of the Virgin and the Life of Christ for the Arena Chapel in Padua.

The Arena Chapel sets this scene on a barren slope above which several angels fly. This scene, depicted in Mantegna’s The Dead Christ, shares some overlap with the Pieta but includes more mourners at Mary’s side.

bonuses.leadin: Unlike astronomers, artists tend not to care as much about the plausibility of their depictions of the Star of Bethlehem. For 10 points each:
parts: In this painter’s version of The Adoration of the Magi, he depicted the Star of Bethlehem as Halley’s comet, and this painter included works like The Lamentation and The Kiss of Judas in his frescoes at the Scrovegni Chapel.

This painter depicted the title Star of Bethlehem as an angel in one of his paintings depicting the Adoration of the Magi. This Pre-Raphaelite depicted a woman descending The Golden Stairs, and in another work by this artist, a character is trapped in a hawthorn bush while Nimue reads spells to him.
answers: Sir Edward Coley Burne-Jones [prompt on Burne or Jones alone; the unnamed painting is The Beguiling of Merlin]

bonuses.leadin: Some scholars theorize that the figures looking over the bridge in Rogier van der Weyden’s St. Luke Drawing the Virgin are these two people, who embrace each other on a bridge in front of a walled city in another work. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Biblical couple whose lives are shown in six panels that sit opposite the Life of the Virgin in the Scrovegni Chapel. The most common depictions of these people show them meeting at the Golden Gate.

This artist painted the panels of the Scrovegni Chapel, also called the Arena Chapel, whose ceiling he filled with a rich cobalt blue. This student of Cimabue demonstrated his skills to the Pope by drawing a perfect circle.

Giotto’s greatest architectural achievement was his design for this building in Florence. Only its lower level, which is decorated with hexagonal sculptural panels, was completed during Giotto’s lifetime.
answers: Joachim and Anne [or Joachim and Anna; accept answers indicating the parents of the Virgin Mary]
campanile of the Florence Cathedral [or bell tower of the Florence Cathedral, accept answers with "Duomo" in place of "Cathedral"]
answers_sanitized: Joachim and Anne [or Joachim and Anna; accept answers indicating the parents of the Virgin Mary]
Giotto di Bondone
campanile of the Florence Cathedral [or bell tower of the Florence Cathedral, accept answers with "Duomo" in place of "Cathedral"]

bonuses.leadin: This church’s Sassetti Chapel contains a fresco by Domenico Ghirlandaio. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this church, whose altar contained a Maesta by Cimabue showing a blue-robed Mary holding an infant Jesus before that painting was moved to the Uffizi gallery.

This saint is the subject of Ghirlandaio's frescoes in the Sassetti chapel, as well as a Cimabue Madonna with four angels. This patron saint of animals is frequently depicted giving his sermon to the birds.
This painter of a Sermon to the Birds and pupil of Cimabue painted the Kiss of Judas as part of a series of frescoes for the Scrovegni Chapel in Padua.
answers_sanitized: Santa Trinita [or Holy Trinity]
Saint Francis of Assisi [or San Francesco d'Assisi]
Giotto di Bondone [or Giotto di Bondone]

bonuses.leadin: The top left inside wall of this building contains a series of frescos depicting the life of Joachim and Anna, opposite to a series showing the life of Mary. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Paduan building which contains statues depicting the virtues and vices on opposite sides. Its many frescos depict the life of Jesus Christ, including the famous Kiss of Judas.
The Scrovegni Chapel was designed and painted by this early Renaissance artist, whose other works include the Ognissanti Madonna.

The largest and last fresco in the Scrovegni Chapel is an illustration of this Biblical scene, in which souls are sent into heaven or hell. Another famous fresco of this scene was created by Michelangelo for the Sistine Chapel.
answers: The Last Judgment [or The Final Judgment; be lenient with synonyms]

Giotto di Bondone
The Last Judgment [or The Final Judgment; be lenient with synonyms]

bonuses.leadin: Ansanus, Sabinian, Crescentius and Victor, the four patron saints of Siena, kneel towards the central figure in one panel from this painting. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this work originally located in the Cathedral of Siena by the artist of the Rucellai Madonna. It makes heavy use of a gold background.
This other early Renaissance artist painted the Santa Croce frescoes for the Bardi and Peruzzi chapels. He’s best known for his fresco cycle in the Arena Chapel.

Scholars still dispute whether Giotto painted any of the frescoes in a chapel named for this saint. He’s passed out and supported by an angel in a Caravaggio painting, while El Greco depicted him in a gray cloak with a rope extending from it as he prays towards a green book and a skull.
answers: St. Francis of Assisi [or Giovanni Francesco di Bernardone; prompt on Francis]
answers_sanitized: Maesta [or Madonna Enthroned with Saints]
Giotto di Bondone
St. Francis of Assisi [or Giovanni Francesco di Bernardone; prompt on Francis]

parts_sanitized: Identify this work originally located in the Cathedral of Siena by the artist of the Rucellai Madonna. It makes heavy use of a gold background.
This other early Renaissance artist painted the Santa Croce frescoes for the Bardi and Peruzzi chapels. He's best known for his fresco cycle in the Arena Chapel.

Scholars still dispute whether Giotto painted any of the frescoes in a chapel named for this saint. He's passed out and supported by an angel in a Caravaggio painting, while El Greco depicted him in a gray cloak with a rope extending from it as he prays towards a green book and a skull.

bonuses.leadin: Jan van Eyck used this technique to paint John the Baptist and John the Evangelist for the closed panels of the Ghent Altarpiece. For 10 points:
parts: Name this technique, in which an artist paints shades of gray on a two-dimensional surface to represent relief sculpture.

This Italian artist, whose paintings broke from the stylization of Byzantine art, used grisaille to paint personifications of the Vices and the Virtues under the frescoes he painted on the walls of the Arena Chapel.

This artist painted a series of grisaille frescoes about the life of John the Baptist for the Chiostro dello Scalzo in Florence. During the 1510s, Rosso Fiorentino and Jacopo Pontormo trained in his studio.
answers_sanitized: grisaille
Giotto de Bondone
Andrea del Sarto
